sections.title: Dataset Overview

sections.blurb: The autonomous-system graph school traffic crosses to leave the country. Nodes are networks; edges are observed links.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
The AS-level graph for this period contains 18 autonomous systems connected by 22 observed edges, built from 13,524 valid traceroute measurements. Each node is a network operator; each edge is a peering or transit link observed at least once in school traffic. The finer-grained AS-city graph resolves routing through 39 location-specific nodes, of which 2 are Internet Exchange Points: physical facilities where ISPs and content networks peer directly. A high IXP-node count suggests traffic is exchanged at multiple peering locations rather than being funnelled through a single upstream provider.

Graph Nodes Edges Valid measurements
AS-level 18 22 13,524
AS-city 39 48 13,524
- IXP nodes in AS-city graph: 2

The AS-level graph for this period contains 30 autonomous systems connected by 65 observed edges, built from 71,287 valid traceroute measurements. Each node is a network operator; each edge is a peering or transit link observed at least once in school traffic. The finer-grained AS-city graph resolves routing through 94 location-specific nodes, of which 3 are Internet Exchange Points: physical facilities where ISPs and content networks peer directly. A high IXP-node count suggests traffic is exchanged at multiple peering locations rather than being funnelled through a single upstream provider.

Graph Nodes Edges Valid measurements
AS-level 30 65 71,287
AS-city 94 186 77,158
- IXP nodes in AS-city graph: 3

The AS-level graph for this period contains 8 autonomous systems connected by 7 observed edges, built from 109 valid traceroute measurements. Each node is a network operator; each edge is a peering or transit link observed at least once in school traffic. The finer-grained AS-city graph resolves routing through 23 location-specific nodes, of which 2 are Internet Exchange Points: physical facilities where ISPs and content networks peer directly. A high IXP-node count suggests traffic is exchanged at multiple peering locations rather than being funnelled through a single upstream provider.

Graph Nodes Edges Valid measurements
AS-level 8 7 109
AS-city 23 26 109
- IXP nodes in AS-city graph: 2
sections.title: Network Topology Graphs

sections.blurb: Hop counts from schools to domestic versus foreign servers. Fewer hops mean more direct routes.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
Path length counts the intermediate hops, at both the IP and autonomous-system level, that school traffic traverses before reaching its destination. Longer paths generally correlate with higher latency and more opportunities for packet loss. The table below shows mean / median across local (domestic M-Lab server) and international destinations.

Across all destinations this period, traceroutes traverse a mean of 5.6 IP hops and cross 2.2 autonomous systems on average. The mean geographic path distance is 98 km (median 13 km), which reflects the physical routing footprint of school traffic.

(mean / median)

sections.title: Average Path Length: Local vs International

sections.blurb: How often traffic crosses an Internet Exchange Point, where networks peer locally instead of routing abroad.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) are physical locations where networks interconnect and exchange traffic directly, often reducing latency and transit costs. A traceroute is counted as crossing an IXP when at least one hop falls within a known IXP prefix. The crossing fraction $f_{\text{IXP}}$ is defined as the ratio of tests with at least one IXP hop to the total number of tests. Higher values indicate that school traffic benefits from local peering infrastructure.

A crossing rate this high ($f_{\text{IXP}} = 11,431 / 14,075 = 81.2\%$) means the vast majority of school traffic passes through at least one Internet Exchange, suggesting well-developed local peering infrastructure that can reduce reliance on expensive international transit.

Overall: 81.2% of tests cross at least one IXP (11,431 / 14,075)

sections.title: IXP Crossing Analysis

sections.blurb: The foreign countries school traffic transits before reaching its destination. More countries mean more external dependence.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
When a traceroute hop is geolocated or registered to a country other than the origin or destination, that country is a transit country: its infrastructure carries the traffic. Heavy dependence on a single transit country creates geopolitical and reliability risk. Policy changes or outages there can affect all international school connectivity. Two methods are used: IP geolocation of hop addresses, and the registration country of each hop's autonomous system.

By IP geolocation, FR is the dominant transit country, appearing in 100.0% of international tests. A single dominant transit country means that a large share of the country's outbound school traffic is physically routed through foreign infrastructure before reaching its destination.

sections.title: Transit Country Dependency

sections.blurb: How concentrated international routes are across transit providers. High concentration is a resilience risk.

data_source.label: Traceroute
data_source.tip: Derived from the measured route only.

sections.narrative_html:
This measures how centralized a country's international routing is. Chokepoint coverage is the share of international tests whose path includes the single most-traversed transit AS. The egress Herfindahl-Hirschman Index partitions tests by one representative egress operator each and is defined as

$$\mathrm{HHI} = \sum_{i} s_i^2$$

where $s_i$ is operator $i$'s share of tests. $\mathrm{HHI}$ near $1$ means a single operator carries essentially all traffic. We report three egress views: the first observed transit AS, the first AS located outside the country (physical border), and the first foreign-registered AS (ownership). Domestic hops are often unresolved, so the first observed transit can already be a foreign operator.

Chokepoint coverage: 100.0% of international tests traverse the single most-used transit AS (AS21320), indicating severe single-point dependence.

  • Egress, first observed transit: $\mathrm{HHI}=1.000$, top operator AS21320 carries 100.0% of tests
  • Egress, first out-of-country: $\mathrm{HHI}=1.000$, top operator AS21320 carries 100.0% of tests
  • Egress, first foreign-registered: $\mathrm{HHI}=1.000$, top operator AS21320 carries 100.0% of tests
Rank Transit ASN Organization Reg. Tests Coverage
1 AS21320 GEANT IAS NL 25 100.0%

Egress concentration by source ASN (first-transit HHI):

Source ASN Intl n Egress HHI Top egress
AS30844 (Liquid Telecommunication.) 25 1.00 AS21320 (100%)

  • Egress, first out-of-country: $\mathrm{HHI}=0.309$, top operator AS327996 carries 45.0% of tests (24 tests without one)

sections.title: International Transit Concentration

sections.blurb: Packet loss along measured paths, and how it tracks with path length and distance.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
Packet loss is measured at each traceroute hop as the fraction of probes that receive no response. This section examines how loss rates vary with path length (number of AS hops) and geographic distance. Longer, more geographically dispersed paths typically experience higher cumulative loss, but loss concentrated at short paths may indicate access-layer or last-mile issues rather than backbone congestion.

Loss rates are highest for paths traversing 3 AS hops (mean loss 0.1095), and lowest for 2 AS hops (mean loss 0.0690). A clear monotonic increase with path length would implicate backbone transit; a spike at short paths suggests last-mile or access network issues.

Loss Rate by AS Path Length:

AS Count n Median Loss Mean Loss IQR
2 10,371 0.0487 0.0690 [0.0018, 0.1130]
3 3,114 0.0964 0.1095 [0.0461, 0.1494]

Geographically, loss is highest in the 200–1,000 km distance bucket (mean loss 0.1104), pointing to where in the routing chain congestion or quality degradation is most pronounced.

Loss Rate by Geographic Distance:

sections.title: Loss Rate Analysis

sections.blurb: Round-trip time, throughput, and loss per school. Flags the underserved.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
This section profiles connectivity quality at the level of individual schools, aggregating RTT, throughput, and loss metrics per school IP. Schools in the top quartile of both RTT and loss are flagged as underserved, a heuristic that surfaces schools with poor path quality on multiple dimensions simultaneously. These schools may benefit most from targeted infrastructure investment or routing improvements.

Only 1.7% of schools (1 of 59) are underserved (RTT $\geq 26.1$ ms and loss $\geq 0.0799$), suggesting generally adequate path quality across the school population.

  • Schools with $\geq 5$ tests: 59
  • Underserved (top quartile RTT + loss): 1 (1.7%)
  • Thresholds: RTT $\geq 26.1$ ms, loss $\geq 0.0799$

sections.title: Per-School Performance

sections.blurb: How performance shifts by hour and day of week, in local time.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
Network performance for schools often varies systematically with time of day and day of week, reflecting shared infrastructure contention during school hours or business peaks. This section shows how test volume, RTT, throughput, and loss shift across hours and days. Pronounced diurnal patterns can indicate congestion during peak hours; weekend vs. weekday differences may reflect shared community or campus network usage.

Day-of-week and hour-of-day are in local time (Africa/Nairobi).

RTT variation across the day is modest (9.7 ms peak-to-trough), suggesting relatively stable path quality regardless of time of day.

Hour-of-Day (local time):

  • Peak usage: 20:00 (992 tests); quietest: 04:00 (38)
  • Median RTT: best at 05:00 (15.8 ms), worst at 20:00 (25.5 ms)

sections.blurb: Route stability and health over the period.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
Path quality encompasses several complementary metrics. Reachability measures whether traceroutes successfully reach the destination AS; gaps indicate routing black-holes or firewall drops. The geographic detour ratio $\rho = d_{\text{path}} / d_{\text{gc}}$ compares the total geolocated hop distance $d_{\text{path}}$ to the straight-line great-circle distance $d_{\text{gc}}$ between the school and server; $\rho = 1.0$ is a perfectly direct route, and larger values indicate circuitous routing (tromboning). Path stability captures how often the same school-to-server pair uses different AS-level routes across repeated tests.

Reachability is high at 95.8%, meaning nearly all traceroutes successfully reach the destination AS. This indicates reliable forwarding across observed paths.

Reachability: 95.8% of traceroutes reach the destination ASN

Server Reachability n
dub01 100.0% 25
nbo01 95.8% 14,050

The mean detour ratio $\rho = 6.22$ is substantially above 1.0, indicating that school traffic travels roughly 6.2$\times$ the great-circle distance. This level of circuitousness commonly arises when traffic exits and re-enters the country through distant peering points.

Geographic Detour Ratio (path distance / great-circle distance):

  • Overall: mean=6.22, median=1.00
  • A ratio of 1.0 = perfectly direct; higher = more circuitous routing
Server Mean Detour Median Detour n
dub01 1.09 1.09 25
nbo01 6.23 1.00 14,050

Path stability is high: 82.5% of school-server flows use a single consistent AS path, and the mean number of distinct paths per flow is 1.19. Stable routing simplifies troubleshooting and suggests well-established peering relationships.

Path Stability (school→server flows with $\geq 3$ tests):

  • Flows analyzed: 63
  • Mean distinct AS paths per flow: 1.19
  • Flows with a single stable path: 82.5%

Reverse Path: 7 / 14,075 measurements have reverse traceroute (0.0%)

Reachability stands at 90.2%. A meaningful fraction of traceroutes do not reach the destination AS, which may reflect ICMP rate-limiting at intermediate hops or routing issues on specific server paths.

Reachability: 90.2% of traceroutes reach the destination ASN

The mean detour ratio $\rho = 10.01$ is substantially above 1.0, indicating that school traffic travels roughly 10.0$\times$ the great-circle distance. This level of circuitousness commonly arises when traffic exits and re-enters the country through distant peering points.

  • Overall: mean=10.01, median=1.00

Path stability is moderate: 63.7% of flows use a single AS path, with an average of 1.56 distinct paths per flow. Some churn in routing paths may reflect load balancing or peering changes.

Path Stability (school→server flows with $\geq 3$ tests):

sections.title: Path Quality

sections.blurb: Where latency accumulates: domestic access versus international transit.

data_source.label: Traceroute + NDT
data_source.tip: Combines the route with NDT speed-test metrics (RTT, throughput, loss).

sections.narrative_html:
RTT decomposition attributes total round-trip latency to individual transit autonomous systems and countries by computing the incremental delay added at each hop. This pinpoints which network operators or geographic segments contribute the most to observed latency. The detour ratio $\rho = d_{\text{path}} / d_{\text{gc}}$ (path distance over great-circle distance) contextualises whether latency stems from physical distance or routing inefficiency.

The single largest RTT contributor is AS36914, adding a mean of 2.4 ms per traceroute (19.1% of total observed RTT). Identifying the operator and location of this AS can guide peering negotiations or routing policy changes to reduce latency for schools.
